data.title: DLocal Q1: Payment Volume Surge Offsets Take Rate Concern
data.description:
DLocal Limited (NASDAQ:DLO) reported strong Q1 results on the 14th of May. The emerging market -focused payment processor showed accelerated momentum in payment volume, carrying onto the bottom row through disciplined cost control. Although the market has been highly concerned with DLocal’s take rate performance, and the quarter continued to show pressure in the metric, DLocal’s TPV expansion is successfully strengthening earnings regardless.

I remained at a Buy rating for DLocal in my previous March article on the stock, “DLocal: Take Rate Disappointment Isn't A Disaster”, after a relatively weak Q4 report. After a very positive reaction to Q1 results, DLocal’s stock has since returned 21% at the time of writing, above S&P 500’s return of 1%.

Despite Take Rate Decline, DLocal’s Q1 Was Successful

DLocal finally showed quite a clear beat of Wall Street’s expectations in Q1. Revenues reached $216.8 million with accelerated year-on-year growth of 18%, coming in $6.9 million above Wall Street’s consensus. The EPS of $0.15 beat by $0.04.

Total payment volume came in increasingly strong, growing by 53% year-on-year to $8.11 billion in USD. The growth in total payment volume even accelerated from 45% in 2024 based on incredibly strong cross-border transactions in especially remittances, commerce, financial services, and streaming. As TPV growth best represents DLocal’s operational growth momentum, the reported TPV figure is highly encouraging regarding DLocal’s future growth. DLocal’s momentum was shown broadly across most verticals and markets.

As can be expected, the financial performance does still reflect a weaker net take rate. Gross profit grew by 35% year-on-year to $84.9 million, well below the reported level of growth in TPV. The take rate declined by approximately 14 basis points year-on-year to 1.05%, also reflecting a continued 4 basis point decrease sequentially. There were several reasons for the decrease - gross profit was pressured significantly from increased processing costs in Nigeria and South Africa, and incremental one-off processing costs in Brazil and a shift to the Payment Orchestrator model in the market. Weakness from one large advertising merchant alone caused a 5 basis point headwind, offsetting great take rate gains in Argentina and from great cross-border transaction growth.

I believe that the commercial performance was very good despite take rate pressure. While Brazil again had a weak quarter, Argentina and other Latin American markets showed very strong momentum. Gross profit also grew well in Egypt. Through very rapid broad-based expansion, total TPV growth outpaces DLocal’s factors pressuring the take rate. The encouraging trend in cross-border transactions should at least partially support the take rate if the growth trend continues into the rest of 2025. Ultimately, I expect that the take rate will see further pressure in upcoming quarters as well, but that accelerated payment volume growth will still trickle down to significant growth in gross profit. The notation of some one-off processing costs supports a thesis of take rate stabilization.

Disciplined cost control caused earnings to perform very well; DLocal is capitalizing well on gross profit growth. With a sequential reduction in general and administrative, and tech expenses, adjusted EBITDA reached $57.9 million with impressive 57% year-on-year growth. While DLocal is still setting up the organization for continued growth, the company is simultaneously executing on operating efficiency initiatives, driving significant margin gains from growth.

Signifying DLocal’s good earnings, the company announced an annual dividend program totaling 30% of annual cash flow. The company opted for a dividend instead of additional share buybacks to protect the stock’s liquidity, being an understandable choice. Shareholders ultimately have the option to increase equity ownership by allocating dividends back towards DLO stock purchases.

DLO Remains Significantly Undervalued

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for DLocal’s stock for an updated fair value. Based on accelerating TPV growth and disciplined cost control, I have raised my revenue expectations to a 2024-2034 CAGR of 14.7% and EBIT margin expectations to an eventually reached level of 24%. DLocal should continue to convert earnings well into free cash flow.

The estimates put DLocal’s fair value at $16.49, 42% above the stock price at the time of writing; DLocal remains an attractive investment based on rapid TPV growth, as the market is still overly concerned over DLocal's take rate decline. The DCF model still expects the company’s take rate to continue to decline based on the current payment volume growth trend.

The fair value estimate is up from $15.35 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 12.02%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I still don't expect DLocal to leverage debt. For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.50% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 9.63% is Professor Aswath Damodaran's most recent January estimate average for Brazil, Mexico, and Nigeria. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.75. With a liquidity premium of 0.3%, the cost of equity and WACC both stand at 12.02%.

The Same Risk Remains

As in previous articles, DLocal’s stock remains risky. The take rate decline is still the largest concern regarding DLocal, and the noteworthy impact of just a handful of merchants makes the take rate trend quite challenging to predict. A continued significant decline could hamper earnings growth significantly once again. As already said, based on current trends, I still believe that payment volume growth should more than offset the base scenario take rate decline.

Takeaway

DLocal reported a successful start to 2025, underlined by accelerated payment volume growth across several markets and especially good momentum in cross-border transactions. While the take rate continued to decline, gross profit expanded rapidly regardless, trickling down to earnings through DLocal’s effective cost management. The company is positioned to continue rapid earnings growth after Q1. As the valuation still undermines DLocal’s growth runway, I remain with a Buy rating for DLO despite the lingering take rate risk.

data.title: Building A SCHD Inspired Dividend Income Screener For May
data.description:
Cloning a popular system

Most of us on Seeking Alpha know by now that the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) is one of, if not the most popular dividend funds in existence. For myself personally, it has become my third-largest core holding in my "core satellite" strategy anchored by index funds. Again, this fund is not so much about alpha, it's about preservation of capital and consistent dividend CAGR over time. I would argue that when looking under the microscope at a multitude of dividends and income-centric funds, SCHD has the smoothest line in dividend CAGR of the bunch, averaging about 11% per annum.

This system, based on the Dow Dividend 100 index, is screened based on the following fundamental basics:

Stocks are ranked by 4 fundamental values:

  • Free cash flow to total debt.
  • Return on equity.
  • FWD yield.
  • 5-year dividend growth rate.

Of course, how they screen for all these things is proprietary; thus, my experiment here today is to attempt to screen for a top 10 stock list using my own screen, trying to match the criteria of the Dow Dividend 100. If my list/screen gets at least a 50% hit rate in my top ten that are held within SCHD, then I believe monthly checkups on this screen going forward are a worthwhile endeavor.

Ranking system

Firstly, let me describe the popular long/short factor linking methodology, the type described in detail by Joel Greenblatt and James O'Shaughnessy. When you are trying to configure a portfolio sleeve to an idea, try to gather as many factors as possible that you can link together, where the end score equals something that is more positive, the better.

A simple example would be positive percentage linking. In a case like this, dividend yield % + 5-year dividend CAGR might make a valuable score in a dividend growth screen. So, a 5% yield plus a 5% CAGR would be a score of 10. Pretty straightforward. A short portfolio would look for the lowest scores, and a long a high-scoring list.

Free cash flow to total debt

The first element of the SCHD screen is the free cash flow to total debt screen. This is the most challenging one to score as it is atypical in that the less debt, the better, and the more free cash flow, the better. What I do here is divide free cash flow by total debt. The higher the percentage of free cash flow as a percentage of total debt, the better. Example: total debt $70,000, FCF $7,000 = Free cash flow is 10% of total debt. This would equal a factor score of 10.

To make sure I wasn't getting a bunch of heavily indebted companies, I set the current ratio to 1 or more to start. The FCF to debt percentage was calculated manually thereafter.

Return on equity

Return on equity is pretty simple, this is already a more positive the better element that can be directly inserted into the equation. My screen used a minimum ROE of 10%.

FWD yield

For this screen, I set 2% as the minimum dividend yield.

5-year dividend growth rate

For this screen, I set a minimum 5% CAGR trailing 5-year dividend growth rate.

Only mid and large cap

This fits with the cap rate structure of SCHD.

Only listed on the NYSE or NASDAQ

This gets me mostly US-based dividend stocks, the goal of the index.

Not market cap weighted

SCHD is market-cap weighted. The results here will not be sorted by market cap, simply by the highest factor scoring stocks in the screen, ultimately whittled down to the top 10.

Sorted by yield

Similar to what the SCHD manager has stated, the final screen is sorted by yield from highest to lowest. In this case, I took the top 20 yields from the screen. The total stocks that passed the screen were 115 stocks.

Negative free cash flow disqualified

I had to take an extra step outside of the screen to divide total debt by free cash flow as noted. If the company had a negative TTM free cash flow, it was disqualified for the sake of this ranking system.

The scorecard

Portfolio data and results

  • EOG Resources (EOG): yield 3.55%: 5YR CAGR:24.96%: In SCHD Y/N : Y
  • InterParfums Inc. (IPAR) : 2.67%: 5YR CAGR: 21.43% : In SCHD Y/N : Y
  • Kontoor Brands (KTB):3.01%: 5YR CAGR: 3.96% : In SCHD Y/N : N
  • Merck & Co. (MRK):4.17%: 5YR CAGR:7.39% : In SCHD Y/N : Y
  • Snap-on Incorporated (SNA):2.7%: 5YR CAGR: 14.53% : In SCHD Y/N : N
  • Nike (NKE): 2.72%: 5YR CAGR: 10.6% : In SCHD Y/N : N
  • Next Star Media Group (NXST): 4.53% : 5YR CAGR: 29.4% : In SCHD Y/N: Y
  • Tenaris S.A. (TS): 6.66% : 5YR CAGR: 10.32% : In SCHD Y/N : N
  • Radian Group (RDN): 3.01% : 5YR CAGR: 49.51% : In SCHD Y/N : Y
  • ConocoPhillips (COP): 3.96% : 5YR CAGR: 16.56% : In SCHD Y/N : Y

Average yield: 3.7%

5-year average dividend CAGR: 18.86%

Number of holdings on screen in SCHD: 6/10, a 60% hit rate

I was very happy with the results, as my ranking system resulted in a more than 50% hit rate for holdings within SCHD, this screen must be a valid scoring, screen in my opinion.

As SCHD reconstitutes each March, this will give me an interesting perspective of where the best-performing or cheapest stocks in SCHD are located on a monthly basis, following their formula. Most of us just browse the top 10 of a given fund, and SCHD is market-cap weighted, so some of the best stocks beyond the top 10 market-cap companies are rarely seen unless you download the full holdings list.

10-year possible yield on cost if averages maintain 80%/70%/60% of CAGR

Below are possible scenarios holding this portfolio for 10 years:

3.7% growing at 15.09% per year for 10 years would have a 15.08% yield on cost at year 10 if the growth rate can maintain 80% of its current average.

3.7% growing at 13.2% per year for 10 years would have a 12.78% yield on cost at year 10 if the growth rate can maintain 70% of its current average.

3.7% growing at 11.31% per year for 10 years would have a 10.8% yield on cost at year 10 if the growth rate can maintain 60% of its current average.

Again, with the goal of SCHD to be dividend growth, high yield, high ROE and ample interest coverage by free cash flow, these are some estimates of where this 10 holding list might end up in a decade should it maintain both its yield and 80/70/60% of its current trailing 5 year dividend CAGR.

Observations

A strong screen for oil and gas. Although only 3 oil & gas related stocks made the final grade (TS, EOG, COP), the total screen before paring down contained quite a lot. With oil in a downtrend, this has hurt the SCHD total return as of late. However, natural gas is probably going to be a future fuel for data center growth, and I do not have faith that small nuclear reactors will ever become as big of a thing as they could. There are just too many issues moving that much uranium around the world to meet the demand, with all the military tracking systems set up to ping movements in radiation.

The stocks passing the screens are natgas or refinery-related in large part. Refiners also should do better than upstream at lower oil prices with reduced input costs [although reduced gasoline prices as well]. Margins should hold up better for them than drillers, in my opinion.

EOG continues to be one of the strongest dividend growth stocks on all metrics every time I run a factor test.

The top 3 scoring companies

EOG Resources: EOG

This US-based oil & gas exploration company has been on a dividend growth tear the past few years. We can see from the above that the company still trades below all 10 year profitability markers for revenue, free cash flow and normalized diluted earnings per share growth.

Although a dividend reduction is expected in upcoming quarters with the headwinds in energy, it still remains one of the biggest dividend growth stories over the past decade at a 19.64% CAGR. The company also has the highest free cash flow to total debt score of the lot; thus they are also the least leveraged of the group.

Interparfums: IPAR

This global fragrance and luxury brand company can be thought of as a poor man's LVMH. They are US-based and own worldwide brands such as Coach, Jimmy Choo, Montblanc, Lacoste, Kate Spade, Ferragamo, and Van Cleef & Arpels. While LVMH has reported a drop-off in demand, that demand is bound to go somewhere and luxury brands that are a level or two below LVMH is probably where they will go.

Just like EOG, this has been a huge dividend growth story flying under the radar the past decade, compounding at 20% per year. The company has the second-highest total free cash flow to total debt score, also indicating a high level of balance sheet safety.

Kontoor Brands Inc.: KTB

This spin-off from a previous dividend king in VFC, designs and manufactures popular denim brands like Wrangler, Lee, and Rock & Republic. Similar to the situation with Interparfums and LVMH, this is a more affordable alternative to Levi's or other more expensive denim brands. While revenue and EPS have been basically flat during this period of existence, free cash flow has grown above the price growth line, which is the most important growth item as it relates to cash to distribute for dividends.

While the company only has a 3.96% dividend CAGR over the past 5 years, they have the highest return on equity of the group. Much of the return of capital to shareholders has been in the form of buybacks in addition to dividends for the 3 years previous.

Risks and summary

As noted, I plan to revisit this screen monthly and hopefully provide an update. Some stocks that would never be considered in a screen like this may pass the test in the forward months if their prices fall and yields rise. Nike is a prime example that now has a very attractive yield and would have never been considered for a higher yield dividend growth portfolio in past years.

This will also allow us to observe secular trends and see if past data in stocks like the oil and gas stocks that screen well for the Dow Dividend 100, deteriorate in the months to come.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Arbor Realty:Q1 Dividend Cut Foreshadows Continued Pressure
data.description:
Q1 recap

I last analyzed Arbor Realty Trust (NYSE:ABR) stock back in Feb 2025. That article was titled "Arbor Realty: This Chart Shows It's Time To Buy”. That article discussed ABR's risk premium relative to treasury rates and concluded with a buy rating:

Since my last writing, a few new catalysts have developed and improved ABR's risk/reward profile materially. The top catalysts are interest rates and also ABR’s latest dividend declaration. These changes have pushed ABR’s yield spread relative to risk-free rates to the thickest levels in at least a decade, indicating unusually favorable risk premiums. These changes have ended the yield curve inversion, thus providing a macroeconomic tailwind for ABR.

Since then, a few new catalysts have evolved around ABR stock. In the rest of this article, I will focus on the top 2 as I see it: the earnings update the company provided for fiscal Q1 2025 and also its latest dividend declaration (which is a substantial dividend cut). Given the crucial role of the dividend in the stock’s return profile, these changes have led me to downgrade the stock to HOLD.

ABR announced its earnings report ("ER") for Q1 2025 on May 2. As you can see from the following screenshot, the ER revealed a set of financials that are quite concerning. To wit, its EPS Normalized came in at $0.28, slightly missing the estimate by $0.01. The GAAP EPS was even lower at $0.16 and missed expectations by an even large margin of $0.05. Revenue of $75.44 million also missed the consensus estimated. Looking ahead, the upcoming quarter's earnings are anticipated to be announced on August 1, 2025. Over the last 90 days, there have been no upward revisions and two downward revisions to the EPS estimates.

Next, I will explain why I see good reasons behind these negative revisions.

Dividend cut

The most important reason on my list is the dividend cut. As seen in the table below, ABR stock has been paying a regular quarterly dividend of $0.4300 per share in recent years. However, its most recent dividend declared for May 2025 is $0.3000 per share (with an ex-dividend date of May 16, 2025). This translates into a large decrease of about 40% compared to previous distributions. Such a sizable cut foreshadows earnings pressure ahead. Key headwinds reflected in its weak Q1 include lower NII (net interest income) and agency loan originations in my view. I expect these pressure to persist given the current macroeconomic conditions and I will elaborate more on this in the risk section.

ABR’s high dividend yield used to be a key consideration behind my earlier buy rating (and also many other analyst’s bullish arguments). The above cut has materially weakened this consideration. To better contextualize this discussion, the chart provided below shows ABR historical dividend yield in the past 10 years. As seen, before the cut, the stock was yielding almost 16%, the peak level in at least one decade. However, after the cut, the forward dividend yield is “only” 11.1% when annualized at the new payout. It is still a double-digit yield, but it is only mildly above its long-term average dividend yield of 9.28% as seen, signaling a much less attractive return/risk ratio compared to that at the time of my last writing.

Book value

On the positive side, the headwinds in the past quarter (e.g., lower NII and agency loan originations) have impacted the ABR’s peer group too and ABR is one that suffered less book value ("BV") damage. To wit, the next chart (published by ABR as part of its ER) compares the change in BV per Share ("BVPS", and also the dividend percentage change as aforementioned) for ABR against its peer average. As seen, ABR is the only company in the sub-sector with sustained book value growth in the past quarter. In contrast, the peer average BVPS has significantly decreased by 43% over the same period.

The stability of its BV, combined with the price pullback in the past quarter, has brought ABR valuation to an attractive level. For example, the next chart shows ABR Price to Book Value (P/BV) ratio in the past 10 years since 2015. Over this period, the P/BV ratio has fluctuated widely, reaching a high of 1.840 and a low of 0.434. But the average P/BV ratio is quite close to 1 (it is 1.107 to be exact), which makes good sense in my model for mREIT stocks. The current P/BV of 0.874 is thus both below the BV and also below its historical average, signaling an attractive entry point for long-term holding.

Other risks and final thoughts

To revisit a point I mentioned earlier, I expect the profit pressure ABR experienced in the past quarter to persist given the current macroeconomic parameters. A key parameter is the delinquency rate on single-family residential mortgages. To be more explicit, the chart below shows the rate booked in domestic offices by all commercial banks. As of the latest observation for Q4 2024, the delinquency rate stands at 1.77% as seen. It is not alarming by itself as a rate of 1.77% is quite mild by history standard as you can see from the second chart below. The second chart plots the same data, just over a broader time period.

What is concerning to me is the trend. The delinquency rate has been on a consistent upward trend throughout 2024 as shown. I am worried that the delinquency rate would keep its climb in the near future given the ongoing inflation, the potential of higher tariffs, and also the rising delinquency rates on other consumer debts such as credit card and auto loans. These issues can keep their pressure, or even further worsen, the NII and origination headwinds for ABR.

To recap, this follow-up analysis on ABR stock is promoted by its Q1 earnings report and also the latest dividend cut. My overall assessment is that these changes have materially shifted its return/risk curve, from an attractive one at the time of my last writing to a neutral one under current conditions. As such, its rating is downgraded to HOLD from my earlier buy rating.

data.title: Retail Sales Revised Higher
data.description:
Real Retail Sales were higher than many expected, compounded by prior months being revised higher. The current pace appears to be tracking above the trend prior to COVID in 2012. One of the elements of this series is that it measures only 80% of total retail sales, since only the larger multi-sited business data is captured. The anomaly-spike post-COVID was a result of retail sales being forced into the reporting businesses, as many smaller retail businesses were forced to close. These have either reestablished themselves, or newer businesses have been started to replace those that have been lost. April 2024 now appears in hindsight to have been roughly when these non-reporting businesses regained their traction by examining the chart patterns.

This report looks healthy, even though pessimists believe a non-repeatable tariff effect is at work. Looking at all the economic data trends, one is struck by the repeated pessimism that the data is not as “bad” as it should be if one’s primary view is shaped by the manufacturing PMI or the Regional Fed Diffusion Indices. Many pull out declines within the data to underline their pessimism. That can only continue so long if the data continues to outdistance expectations.

I remain very, very positive on the economy and selected equities.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: First Merchants Corporation: Downgrading After A Good Run
data.description:
One bank that has had a pretty good run in the time in which I have been bullish about it has been First Merchants Corporation (NASDAQ:FRME). Back in June of 2024, I decided to rate the institution a ‘buy’. This was based primarily on the valuation of the company, combined with the continued growth it had experienced in key areas of its balance sheet. Asset quality wasn't great, but it certainly wasn't awful. At the end of the day, I believed that it would have market beating potential though, mostly because of its valuation.

So far, that has played out well. From the time I rated the company a ‘buy’ through the present day, shares have enjoyed upside of 27.5%. That's well above the 7% increase that the S&P 500 enjoyed. Unfortunately, all good things must come to an end. And my bullishness about the institution is no exception. In recent years, financial performance has been admittedly mixed. Even as the balance sheet grows where it matters most, revenue and profits have experienced some unwelcomed volatility. Even though the company's return on assets remain high, shares are not as cheap as I would like them to be. When you add all of this together, I believe that downgrading it to a ‘hold’ makes the most sense.

Time to step back

As someone who was born and raised in Ohio, I always like to talk about companies that have some exposure to my state. Although First Merchants Corporation is headquartered out of Indiana and has most of its 111 banking centers in that state, it does also have six branches out of central Ohio. As the image below illustrates, the company has some exposure to Michigan as well. But again, Indiana is where it's focused the most.

From its locations, First Merchants Corporation engages in a wide array of traditional banking activities. On the commercial side of things, it provides small business loans, engages in asset-based lending activities, provides treasury management services and merchant processing services, and more. On the consumer side, the company accepts deposits and provides certain online banking, mobile banking, and in person banking services. The company has a healthy mortgage banking arm, and it boasts a private wealth advisory operation that has expertise in investment banking, private banking, financial planning, and more.

One of the really good things about the institution is that it continues to grow from a balance sheet perspective. From 2022 through 2024, deposits at the bank expanded from $14.38 billion to $14.52 billion. Unfortunately, there was a slight decline to $14.46 billion as of the end of the most recent quarter, which would be the first quarter of 2025. Over the same window of time, we have seen loans grow from $11.78 billion to $12.66 billion. And as of the end of the most recent quarter, there was a further increase to $12.81 billion.

As the image above illustrates, the company is quite diversified from a loan perspective. The greatest exposure for the institution would be the commercial and industrial loans that account for 33.1% of its overall portfolio. However, it does also have significant exposure to residential mortgages, commercial real estate non-owner-occupied, and commercial real estate owner-occupied properties. Another interesting trait about the firm's loan portfolio is that only 32% of it falls under the fixed rate category. The rest is some form of variable loan. While this might be positive in a rising interest rate environment, that seems unlikely to be what the future has in store for us. In all likelihood, if the company keeps this same exposure, it will see some weakness in income once interest rates start dropping.

Naturally, there are other aspects of the company's balance sheet that deserve attention. Take, if you will, the value of securities. These have actually been dropping consistently as management focuses more on building up its loan portfolio. At the end of the most recent quarter, the firm had $3.43 billion in securities on its books. This was down from the $3.46 billion reported the previous year. And it was down quite a bit from the $4.26 billion that the institution had at the end of 2022. Cash and cash equivalents have been stuck in a pretty big range. At the end of the most recent quarter, they totaled $417.6 million. That was up from the $386.5 million reported the previous year. At the same time, the company did see overall debt grow from $1.16 billion to $1.34 billion.

When it comes to the income statement, I wish things were better. After seeing net interest income rise from $503.4 million in 2022 to $541.9 million in 2023, there was a drop to $485.4 million last year. Despite the growing balance sheet of the company, it was negatively affected by a reduction in its net interest margin from 3.41% to 3.19%. In the most recent quarter, however, the $126.1 million that the company generated beat out, ever so slightly, the $125.1 million reported at the same time last year.

Non-interest income has, fortunately, been on the rise. Even though there were certain one-time items such as a $20.8 million realized loss on sales of available for sale securities last year and a $20 million gain on the sale of one of its branches, the increase from $105.6 million in 2023 to $125.6 million last year was largely attributable to net gains and fees on the sale of certain loans on its books. As I mentioned earlier, the company does have some other operations that are noteworthy. This includes its wealth advisory business that brought in $34.2 million in fiduciary and wealth management fees last year, and its card payment activities that brought in $19.3 million. For the most recent quarter, we saw an increase to $30 million in non-interest income compared to the $26.6 million reported the previous year. This improvement came largely from a jump in net gains and fees on the sale of certain loans from $3.3 million to $5 million.

Where the company has struggled the most in recent years has been when it comes to net profits. From 2022 through 2024, net income actually contracted from $220.7 million to $199.5 million. Fortunately, the first quarter of this year saw an increase to $54.9 million compared to the $47.5 million reported last year. Obviously, the growth in revenue helped. But also helpful was a drop in non-interest expense from $96.9 million to $92.9 million it seems to have been largely from a decline in salaries and employee benefits costs, a drop in outside data processing fees, and some other miscellaneous categories.

The weakness on the bottom line has played a role in my decision to be more neutral about the business. This is not to say that the company is a bad one though. Shares, for instance, really are not that expensive relative to earnings. In the chart above, you can see the company stacked up against five similar banks using the price to earnings approach. With a reading of 11, the company is higher than the 6 to 10 range that I aim for. But it's not materially higher. It's also cheaper than all of the other five banks that I compared it to in that chart. In the chart below, you can see both its price to book and price to tangible book multiples. In both of these cases, you can see that two of the five banks were cheaper than our candidate is. But with a price to book multiple of only 0.98, First Merchants Corporation is still what I would consider to be a bit cheap.

When it comes to asset quality, the picture is mixed, but not bad. In the first chart below, you can see its return on assets compared to the same five banks. With a reading of 1.21%, First Merchants Corporation is of a higher quality than is needed to be attractive to me. I typically look for a return on assets reading of at least 1%. In fact, using this approach, First Merchants Corporation is actually better than four of the five banks I compared it to. In the chart below that, however, you can see the same type of analysis using its return on equity. The 9.38% reading that we get here is just marginally below the 10% minimum that I like to see. However, three of the five banks I compared it to ended up being superior to it.

Takeaway

All things considered, First Merchants Corporation is not a bad candidate. But I'm not looking to generate market returns. My goal in investing is to beat the market. Given that the stock has gotten more expensive and considering some of the problems that has had from a profitability perspective, I believe that it would be prudent to downgrade it to a ‘hold’ at this time.

data.title: The Unusual Effects Of Anavex's Blarcamesine On Alzheimer's Disease (Upgrade)
data.description:
I continue to reassess the mechanisms of action of blarcamesine and the causes of Alzheimer’s disease itself based on Anavex’s (NASDAQ:AVXL) long-term open label trial results. Blarcamesine appears to perform no better than Aricept for mild Alzheimer’s disease and slightly worse than the historical placebo decline for moderate Alzheimer’s disease (table three). And yet it performs considerable better than both when begun in mild Alzheimer’s disease (or mild cognitive impairment). Or put another way, blarcamesine is largely ineffective against mild Alzheimer’s disease and ineffective against moderate Alzheimer’s disease, but is effective from mild Alzheimer’s disease through moderate Alzheimer’s disease (see tables below). That may be enough for it to be conditionally approved by the European Medicines Agency, likely by the end of this year (under this scenario blarcamesine can be marketed, but further trials will be needed).

Many of the following figures are based off of charts and/or extrapolations so they are not exact, but the patterns are more important than the precise numbers. Note also an ADAS-Cog13 number of 34.35 marks the beginning of moderate Alzheimer’s disease (60.9-MMSE of 20 X 1.85=ADAS-Cog11 of 22.9 X 1.5=ADAS-Cog13 of 34.35).

Early Start Blarcamesine Aricept

Baseline 28.6 27.4

One Year 32.8 31.4

Two Years 37.7 37.4

Three Years 41.2 44.4

Four Years 46.7 52.4

Late Start Blarcamesine **Aricept Placebo

Baseline 30.4 30.9 31

*One Year 36.4 36.6 37

Two Years 41.4 42.9 46

Three Years 46.4 51 53.5

Four Years 51.4 60 61.5

*During the first year about 65% of the late start group were on other Alzheimer’s medications. None were on blarcamesine.

**Other acetylcholinesterase inhibitors were also part of this study, but the authors did not find a major difference between their effects.

Explaining these results may get to the heart of understanding Alzheimer’s disease itself. The following diagram helps in this explanation.

Sigma-1 receptor agonists like blarcamesine and Aricept inhibit intracellular calcium release from the endoplasmic reticulum. In doing so, they inhibit acetylcholinesterase activity. Unlike blarcamesine, Aricept directly inhibits acetylcholinesterase activity but this may not provide much additional benefit. Sigma-1 receptor agonists also limit oxidative stress in the form of hydrogen peroxide, but the main cause of memory impairment during mild Alzheimer’s disease appears not to be oxidative stress but the breakdown of acetylcholine by acetylcholinesterase.

NMDA receptors are the main mediators of oxidative stress in Alzheimer’s disease. They are activated by both diaclyglycerol and intracellular calcium release (chart) with sigma-1 receptors only inhibiting the later. So sigma-1 receptor agonists do not prevent the transition from mild to moderate Alzheimer’s disease (although they come closer to doing so in those with a functioning sigma-1 receptors and in non-APOE4 carriers). Also due in part to oxidative stress, acetylcholinesterase activity declines as Alzheimer’s progresses.

After mild Alzheimer’s disease, the problem becomes the peroxynitrite-mediated oxidation and nitration of critical receptors, transport systems, and enzymes in the brain. This includes the synthesis of acetylcholine, the transport of acetylcholine, and its release from muscarinic acetylcholine receptors (study). Sigma-1 receptor agonists inhibit the production of the oxidant hydrogen peroxide, but most hydrogen peroxide is converted into and replaced by peroxynitrite (ONOO-) in moderate Alzheimer’s disease. At this point, it is necessary to inhibit the formation of peroxynitrite and scavenge it to slow down the further progression of the disease. Blarcamesine appears to do neither or at least not sufficiently to at least temporarily slow down the progression of moderate Alzheimer’s disease.

The tetrahydrofuran derivative blarcamesine, though, does in the presence of copper convert hydrogen peroxide into the end product gamma hydroxybutrate. Gamma hydroxybutrate may decrease oxidative stress in part by inhibiting NMDA receptor activation (study). However, this compound can cause dizziness and confusion. This may be the source of many of the side effects of blarcamesine. Ironically, the individuals responding best to the drug might be the ones experiencing the worst side effects.

The combination of not only inhibiting the formation of hydrogen peroxide, but also scavenging hydrogen peroxide may explain why the benefits of blarcamesine increase over time. Individuals taking blarcamesine begin with less oxidative stress burden during the transition to moderate Alzheimer’s disease than those taking Aricept or those on placebo. They may also benefit from the indirect antioxidant effects of its metabolite gamma hydroxybutrate. Thus they progress more slowly through moderate Alzheimer’s disease.

Again, I believe this should be sufficient for EMA conditional approval. This may be even more likely due to the lack of effective treatments for Alzheimer’s disease.

Here are some of the ways this could go:

A flat out rejection of blarcamesine would be devastating for Anavex and its stockholders. Despite the company's work on other indications, Anavex's stock value would likely drop to between one and two points. A conditional approval should lead to a subtantional increase in Anavex's stock price, although certainly not as much as an outright approval. The results for Anavex's 3-71 for schizophrenia should be known before the EMA's decision, but these results are not likely to move Anavex's stock dramatically in one direction or another. At this point, Anavex's fate is not dependent on financial resources as it still has $115.8 million in cash and cash equivalents as of March 2025 compared to $132.2 million in Septemeber 2024 (financial report). Rather its fate is largely in the hands of the EMA.

The EMA will look at real-world evidence which includes open label trial data in reaching its decision. In open label trials, both the researchers and the participants know that everyone is receiving the drug and not a placebo. Of the problems related to such data, the possibility of bias is probably more important than any potential placebo effect on Alzheimer's disease patients, especially those in the later stages. The EMA will likely ask Anavex for detailed data and explanations along the way (clock stops). Anavex's presentation of data to the EMA will need to be much more precise than it has presented to the public after the last several years. The question is that when it does present detailed data how the EMA will respond. Perhaps, most importantly what separation does the EMA want to see between blarcamesine and the current standard of care in moderate Alzheimer's disease (when all treatments are begun in mild cognitive impairment or mild Alzheimer's disease). Secondarily, how severe and common are the side effects with blarcamesine. I think that blarcamesine's is safe and effective enough to gain EMA conditional approval, but there is no way to be certain.

Therefore, I cautiously recommend buying the stock based on Anavex's results.

data.title: Ormat Technologies: The Pure-Play Geothermal Company Is Having Its Moment
data.description:
In 1904 Prince Piero Ginori Conti, working at a site in the town of Larderello, near Pisa, Italy, was able to light five light bulbs using his experimental generator that harnessed steam coming out of the earth. A decade after that, the first commercial geothermal power plant facility was commissioned nearby, generating 25x as much electricity (250kW), enough to power about 50 modern day homes.

Today, more than 120 years later, Larderello remains a working geothermal site, with 34 power plants operated by Itay’s global utility Enel (OTCPK:ENLAY), with a total capacity of 800MW supplying roughly 2% of Italy’s electricity – or enough to fulfill a small country’s needs (like El Salvador, which also has significant geothermal supply).

Geothermal power generation, at first glance, looks like a perfect solution for cleaning up electricity grids. Using nothing but the heat from the ground below, the technology adds zero emissions and can generate baseload (24/7) regardless of whether it rains, shines or blows outside. It is the only "truly contained" thermal base load power. All other, including nuclear, require fuel arriving from outside the power plant (whether by pipe, truck, train or ship).

Even though geothermal power has been deployed successfully around the world, it remains a niche segment of most grids. Only a few countries rely on geothermal for more than 10% of their electricity needs. But growth in installed capacity has marched along decade after decade. That is because, even though it is not cheap to build, it has the lowest "all-in" lifetime cost out of comparable, low-emission (but dispatchable) alternatives:

As is widely understood, re-configuring the electricity matrix is one of the most important steps in controlling greenhouse gas emissions. This can be done with existing technology, unlike other areas like commercial air travel, shipping and cement production – where no commercial solutions are readily available to significantly reduce emissions.

For electricity grids, the solution will necessarily include all types of zero or low emission technologies, including more nuclear, more CCGT (with carbon capture), more solar, wind and hydro (especially pumped storage upgrades, where possible). And more geothermal.

More of everything renewable will be needed to replace coal, unabated gas and (in emerging markets) fuel oil. And that is before the small issue of growing demand. Whether driven by data centers – in developed world - or growing middle income populations in developing countries, demand for electricity is sure to grow, and likely faster than during the last few decades.

So, we have the building blocks for an investment thesis at hand. We have a niche market (geothermal power) with a practically certain and very long runway for growth (fostered by transition policies). And importantly, a dominant player, with vertical integration and a long track record: Nevada-based Ormat Technologies, Inc. (NYSE:ORA)

ORMAT's Business

The US is a leader in Geothermal generation, with roughly 1/4th of the global installed capacity. According to EIA, The US has 40GW of potential and only 10% has been developed. Most of the resource is in Western states, where Ormat has its HQ and most of their power plants.

If you are doing anything geothermal, anywhere in the world, you will not go far before running into Ormat Technologies. Starting as an EPC contractor 60 years ago, the (originally Israeli company), has become vertically integrated, owning equipment production facilities and power plants. In the US, the leader in geothermal, Ormat is the leading producer (by a hair, but still...).

As of 2024, most of the Ormat's revenue and margin is coming from electricity generation (80% of revenues), with 16% coming from equipment sales and the remaining 4% from battery energy storage systems (OTCQB:BESS).

The +1,200MW power generation assets are mostly in the US (some 200MW are solar PV and recovered heat). On top of these, the construction pipeline includes 116MW of new geothermal projects coming online in the short term (2025-26). Over the medium term, the global installed capacity will grow 400MW to 500MW by 2028.

The BESS business is ramping up to scale as well. The company has a clear project pipeline that will add some 700MW of new storage capacity, all in the US – these are key grid stability assets that don’t add generation but compensate for intermittent renewables like solar and wind. There is a big opportunity in this market for Ormat, helping spread around solar power beyond daylight hours and buy valuable minutes for backing-up wind in Texas.

According to the company’s medium-term plan, which goes out to 2028, EBITDA generation will improve from about +$500 million currently to $800 million, with an incremental capital investment of about $2.2B (implying a reasonable 14% EBITDA margin on incremental capex).

Valuation and Leverage

The current valuation looks steep and is turning off many investors. It earns a solid "F" from the Seeking Alpha crowd. Although the multiples are indeed high, peeling back a layer leads to a more measured interpretation.

The company has a enterprise value of about $7B. comprised of $4.5B market capitalization and $2.5B in net debt. Both have been reasonably stable through the bast three years:

The first conclusion is that debt is not too high nor growing. With EBITDA of around $550 million, and net debt or $2.5B, a 4.2x multiple is not too taxing given the long-term contracted nature of most generation assets. The weighted average (and 90% fixed) rate on debt is below 5%, implying narrow spreads and good quality power offtakers.

The following is a table of current installed capacity and its corresponding replacement cost:

The most important assumption is the $4,500/MW for geothermal. In support, we have the recent example in Ormat's $88 million acquisition of the 20MW plant called Blue Mountain (Nevada), in operation since 2009, at $4,400/MW price.

Assuming the “products segment” which has $120 million in revenues and $300 million backlog is worth around $500 million, the whole company (based in installed capacity replacement value) would be worth $6B.

That makes the $7B valuation look less excessive, but still "toppy". To get comfortable with it, one needs to consider the development pipeline and its likely impact on valuation (not considered in the analysis above).

Planned project execution will take EBITDA into $800 million for 2028, only three years away. The short time means most of the projects are under construction or in advanced permitting already.

At valuation levels similar to today's (and perhaps helped by lower future interest rates), The additional $300 million in EBITDA will create $4B - $3.6B in additional enterprise value at 14x (today’s multiple) or 12x (normalized). Both are higher than the capex of $2.2B. Put another way, the EBITDA multiple would need to compress to 9x on $800 million EBITDA for enterprise value to remain flat.

At a normalized (lower) 12x EBITDA multiple, the 2028 enterprise value would be $9.5B. If net debt remains around $2.5B, the market cap would increase to $7B, a 55% increase from todays $4.5B.

Management explicitly pointed out (during 2024 investors day) that all capex could potentially be funded with internally generated funds, with 2028 debt ratios targeted at 3.0x. I don't expect they will fund growth that way, some debt makes sense and excess cash might be used to purchase shares or increase the dividend (in both cases contributing to returns obviously).

In any case, nice returns are still possible or even likely, depending on how you view the risk around late stage development and construction.

in regards to the current portfolio in the US, I have disregarded an important component - about a fourth (250MW) of capacity is under PPA's that are being renegotiated. If there ever is a perfect moment to be long clean baseload electricity, this might be it. These contracts often go out 10 or more years (as they anchor construction of new infrastructure) and I expect strong pricing and upside to recurring EBITDA (for competitive reasons there is little available to model this, so it is just that - upside).

Recent earnings:

Ormat released Q1 2025 earnings on May 7th. All metrics are pointing toward continues progress toward the stated EBITDA goal. At $150 million in Q1, that would annualize to $600 million, well ahead of last year (and several projects will CoD later this year).

Stellar growth from BESS revenues (+120%) segment is likely to continue, as these deployments scale, so will the related margins, which improved to 30.6% form 7.5% in the Q1 of last year (in Q1 2025, margins were also helped by strong pricing on merchant markets within PJM during winter months).

Going forward, key milestones to watch are around the progress of development milestones, especially the four geothermal projects expected to come online in the remaining part of 2025:

What can go wrong

Well, as noted, debt is not a concern for this independent power producer. The cash flows are highly contracted, and the offtakers are usually investment grade US utilities (like NV Energy, a Berkshire Hathaway Energy company).

With new projects, there is always risk of cost overruns. In early stage developments, the risk of “dry wells” is a recurring possibility (the development pipeline is not dependent on discoveries). Those risks – exploration - are often shared with other parties, like offtakers. The current rush to secure clean power for data centers is giving more leverage to IPPs to negotiate risk sharing mechanisms as well.

The technology has developed slowly for a reason. The high cost of development, due to drilling and exploration is some 50% higher than a combined cycle gas plant (which is efficient and cleaner but not emission free). Geo power plants are economical in high marginal cost systems (emerging markets lacking domestic energy reserves) or with the aid of incentives (in the US).

The world, including the DOE, has been moving decisively toward incentivizing renewables, and that is not expected to change, although more emphasis should be placed on baseload solutions like geothermal.

Like all renewables, you have to go where the resource is. In this case, the resource is found near fault lines of major tectonic plates. That means, similar to wind and solar, transmission infrastructure needs to be put in place. Near term, most of the development pipeline is within existing infrastructure in Nevada and California. Longer term, this limits potential and requieres national policies to align. New Zealand and Indonesia are the main growth opportunities outside the US (Ormat will likely participate mostly as a supplier of equipment and services).

Finally, international clients expose the company to higher credit risk and some FX risk as well. The size of the international footprint is not large (and electricity sales can usually be redirected to another client should an offtaker default).

Conclusion:

Geothermal is almost purpose-built for data centers and hyperscale demand. They need continuous clean power, so much so, that are willing to backstop small nuclear projects or re-start old ones. Geothermal is easier an cheaper than both of those.

Ormat, the worlds leading operator (and equipment supplier), is well positioned in this "niche" technology. Although significant cost reductions and deeper drilling will gradually help bring the technology closer to main-stream, the investment thesis is not dependent on that.

The company currently has very high visibility on reaching $800 million EBITDA. In addition, that EBITDA may have embedded upside from PPA negotiations that are ongoing for about 1/4th of the US generation capacity. Given modest valuation scenarios of around 12x EBITDA, which is lower than prevailing, and ignoring potential upside from PPA renegotiation, there is still about 50% stock appreciation is in the cards by 2028.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Cisco: Low Growth Despite AI Infrastructure Opportunities
data.description:
Cisco Systems, Inc. (NASDAQ:CSCO) reported Q3 2025 earnings that beat Wall Street estimates on revenues and earnings per share. I used to own a small number of shares in Cisco, and the stock had done well for me when it was part of my portfolio, but it was not necessarily impressive. It was more of a name that steadily grew. That also should not be a major surprise given Cisco’s positioning in the market.

In this report, I discuss the company’s most recent earnings, the outlook for Q4 and FY25, and I apply a stock price target to the name.

Cisco Positioned For Support Of AI Hyperscalers

Cisco is not a company that is going to see huge AI growth. That is driven by its significant exposure to the more traditional product line up of switches and routers. That does position the company to see some AI growth from supporting hyperscalers, but the company is not a provider of foundational models. Its AI growth comes more from providing AI infrastructure with some AI-powered analytics and security applications. So, the company can grow its revenues driven by demand from networking solutions for cloud and AI companies.

The company is working with Nvidia (NVDA) to build systems that use Nvidia hardware and Cisco software for use in data centers. The company also has a broader effort to tailor its hardware for AI workload needs. In my view, Cisco is not an AI company in the traditional sense. The company just provides networking solutions in support of AI companies. That does not mean that the company cannot benefit from AI growth, as AI capital expenditures drive networking hardware and software demand and AI is infused in threat detection for the security business, network optimization and telemetry.

Cisco Earnings Beat as Security and Observability Drive Growth

In the third quarter, sales grew 11% to $14.1 billion, beating analyst estimates by $91.5 million. Product sales grew 15% year-on-year while services revenues grew 2.7% year-on-year. Product revenues grew 54%, driven by growth in security while observability sales were up 24%, networking sales were up 8% and collaboration sales were up 4%. The surge in product revenues was largely driven by the acquisition of Splunk which was completed in March 2024.

Margins grew modestly by 30 basis points to 68.6%, and that translated through to the operating income margins, which also grew 30 basis points resulting in a operating come increase slightly above the top-line growth. Core earnings per share of $0.96 beat estimates by $0.04.

Cisco Has A Strong Track Record Of Beating Wall Street Estimates

For the full year, the company expects $56.5 billion to $56.7 billion in sales compared to analyst estimates that have expectations set at the lower end of the range. EPS of $3.77-$3.79 would indicate little year-on-year growth with analysts having set their expectations at the high end of the range. For Q4, Cisco expects sales between $14.5 billion and $14.7 billion pointing at 8% year-on-year growth at the mid-point of the range while earnings per share are expected to be between $0.96 and $0.98. Analysts once again are expecting earnings to come in at the high end of the range.

I don’t believe it provides a substantial risk that analysts have their expectations for EPS set at the high end of the range. The reason is quite simple: Cisco seemingly always beats estimates.

Is Cisco Stock Attractive?

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

While Cisco can definitely capitalize on AI infrastructure demand, it is not expected to be extremely high growth. The CAGR for EBITDA growth towards FY27 is a mere 3.3%. Free cash flow is likely going to be significantly better at 19.9% annually. However, that does not get us much further than a hold rating and a $65.77 price target. This provides 7.7% upside, which is below the returns I am looking for. As a result, I am marking the stock a hold.

Conclusion: Slow Growth Company

Cisco is not necessarily unattractive from earnings potential point of view, and it is working on making its products better suited for AI use. However, if we connect that to the current valuation, it is more of a low growth company rather than a major AI play and that is more than fine, but also means I am rating CSCO stock a Hold.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Walmart Rings Warning Bell On Tariffs
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

WMT execs say suppliers and retailers can’t absorb all the higher costs. (0:15) Wholesale inflaton sinks unexpectedly. (1:11) Happy 85th birthday, McDonald’s! (4:03)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, Walmart (NYSE:WMT) management warned during their earnings call and in interviews that tariffs could lead to higher prices for U.S. consumers.

CFO John David Rainey said: "We’re wired for everyday low prices, but the magnitude of these increases is more than any retailer can absorb. It’s more than any supplier can absorb. And so I’m concerned that consumer is going to start seeing higher prices. You’ll begin to see that, likely towards the tail end of this month, and then certainly much more in June."

CEO Doug McMillon said the company will do its best to keep consumer prices as low as possible, but due to the magnitude of the tariffs, even at the reduced levels announced this week, the company can't absorb all the pressure given the reality of narrow retail margins.

Walmart held off on issuing Q2 EPS and operating income guidance with its report.

The warning on prices wiped out premarket gains seen after Walmart released its Q1 results. Comparable sales in the U.S. rose 4.5% to top the consensus estimate of 3.9%.

It was a busy morning on the macro front

Wholesale inflation fell unexpectedly in April, with the Producer Price Index down -0.5%, compared with the +0.2% expected and flat in March (revised from -0.4%). The annual rate fell to +2.4% from +3.4% prior (revised from +2.7%). That was lower than then +2.5% consensus.

Over 40% of the April decline in the index for final demand services is primarily due to margins for machinery and vehicle wholesaling, which fell 6.1%.

Economist Joseph Brusuelas says: “This is evidence that trade policy is resulting in margin compression, which will then result in consumers absorbing higher prices starting in May.”

Ex food and energy, the core PPI fell -0.4% M/M, as compared to +0.3% consensus, and +0.4% prior (revised from -0.1%). On a yearly basis, it’s up +3.1%, meeting consensus.

Pantheon Macro economists say “CPI and PPI data imply the core PCE deflator rose by 0.12% in April, reducing the inflation rate to 2.5%, from 2.6% in March.”

In addition, retail sales edged up +0.1% M/M in April, a hair above the no change expected and decelerating from the prior month's 1.7% increase (revised from +1.4%),

Excluding motor vehicles and parts, core retail sales also inched up +0.1% from a month earlier, missing the +0.3% consensus and slowing from +0.8% in March (revised from +0.5%).

Janney strategist Guy LeBas says while “retail sales were ‘okay,’ they don't paint a picture of a consumer rushing to buy ahead of tariffs. If anything, retail sales show subtle signs of belt-tightening ahead of employment risks in coming months.”

Wells Fargo economists says: “A key question after this week’s 90-day reprieve from a full-blown trade war with China is whether the sharp lowering in the tariff rate for one of America’s largest trading partners comes quickly enough to blunt the worst of the economic fallout.”

“If tariffs indeed introduced the ‘stagflationary shock’ we have warned about, then an unwinding of those tariffs surely has scope to boost growth and lower inflation, if not in an absolute sense, at least relative to expectations that priced in tariff-induced shocks.”

On top of all that, Fed Chairman Jerome Powell spoke, but kept things focused on its framework review that it embarks on every five years. This time around, communications will be an important focus, chiefly how to convey the degree of uncertainty facing the economy.

Powell did note: “Higher real rates may also reflect the possibility that inflation could be more volatile going forward than in the inter-crisis period of the 2010s. We may be entering a period of more frequent, and potentially more persistent, supply shocks — a difficult challenge for the economy and for central banks.”

In other news of note, McDonald's (MCD) turns 85 years old today. The original McDonald's restaurant was opened by Richard and Maurice McDonald in San Bernardino, California in 1940. The restaurant’s "Speedee Service System" attracted Ray Kroc, who joined as a franchising agent in 1954 and opened the first franchised McDonald’s in Des Plaines, Illinois, in 1955 before buying out the brothers in 1961. By the 1970s, McDonald’s had become a global icon, introducing signature menu items and expanding into international markets.

Looking ahead, McDonald's plans to expand, aiming for over 50,000 locations by 2027. Near term menu innovations include a continuation of the $5 Meal Deal, expansion of Big Arch Burgers, and the McCrispy chicken sandwich and strips going international.

Shares of McDonald's (MCD) have outperformed the S&P 500 Index since 1995 by more than 600 percentage points

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, BMO Capital Markets is taking a victory lap, keeping its S&P 500 year-end target at 6,100 and emphasizing a commitment to a disciplined investment process rather than reacting to market fluctuations.

Strategist Brian Belski says: “Our bullish stance on U.S. stocks has been belittled, doubted and ridiculed so far in 2025 like no other time in our career.”

“Demand for U.S. stocks is and should be robust – period,” he said, adding that this resilience indicates the market is appropriately rewarding quality assets.

“Yes, we revised our year-end forecast in terms of price and earnings just five weeks ago,” Belski said. “However, to clarify, we did maintain our initial 2025 forecast (6,700 + $275) as our bull case for the remainder of the year. So why did we revise lower? Discretion is the better part of valor, and a 30%-plus move higher in the face of profoundly negative momentum, coupled with equally volatile exogenous news flow was a far from prudent recipe for investing.”

Looking ahead, he recommends watching stabilization of earnings alongside returning company guidance, less reactive price movements to Washington news and continued broadly distributed equity performance as signposts for market stability.
